roots.title: Course Announcement and Regression Model Analysis
roots.summary: Lecture 11 introduces the use of JupyterLab for content delivery, providing a link to presentation slides and mentioning the Slido platform for audience engagement. The constant model and linear regression are discussed, focusing on model optimization, the four-step framework for model creation, and the importance of transformations. Key concepts include loss functions (L1, L2, squared loss), optimal parameter estimation, and model performance evaluation, including the Y-intercept, coefficients, predictions, and mean squared error (MSE). The parameterization of the model using TETA is detailed, and the Anzcom Cortet dataset is analyzed for data visualization and model fit evaluation. Additionally, a poll on boba sales predictions is conducted, showing student responses based on previous sales data. The significance of objective functions in estimation models is also covered, illustrating the constant model's approach to predictions using past sales data.

children.title: Constant Model, Linear Regression, and Dataset Analysis
children.summary: The segment introduces the constant model and its optimization in modeling, revisiting the four-step framework for model creation and the relevance of transformations in linear regression. It covers simple linear regression concepts, focusing on model fit through loss functions like L1, L2, and squared loss, and explains the estimation of optimal parameters and evaluation of model performance. Key elements such as the Y-intercept, coefficients, predictions, and mean squared error (MSE) are discussed, emphasizing the importance of defining loss functions in empirical risk. The parameterization of the model using TETA is also detailed. Lastly, the Anzcom Cortet dataset is analyzed, highlighting the need for data visualization to reveal probabilistic characteristics and the evaluation of model fit.

children.title: Introduction to Constant Model and Transformations
children.summary: The lecture introduces the constant model, highlighting its practical application and optimization in modeling. It reviews previously covered concepts, particularly focusing on evaluation and the four-step framework for creating a model. Additionally, it revisits transformations from a prior visualization lecture, discussing their relevance in linear regression. Homework preparation for the next session is also mentioned.

children.title: Introduction to Constant Model and Transformations
children.summary: The lecture introduces a constant model, emphasizing practical application and optimization of models. It will review previously covered concepts, particularly focusing on evaluation and the framework for creating a model, which includes four steps. Additionally, the lecture will revisit transformations discussed in a prior visualization lecture and their relevance in linear regression. Homework preparation for the next session will also be mentioned.

Audio: I'm actually very excited about this lecture. This is a lecture that we're going to actually get some practice, but everything a bit seen in a previous lecture. We're going to practice a lot more. So we are going to see a new model. So it's called a constant model. We're going to talk about Some more last functions we're going to try. Keep optimizing models and then see how this calculus thing that we started using in fitting a model is going to actually be really helpful and then we're going to also just briefly talk about transformation. You had seen transformation before in the visualization lecture, but you're going to again see it one more time and you're going to see how it can be really helpful in linear regression. So this is where we are. So think about this lecture as a like kind of a debrief lecture plus a little bit of examples that we're going to go over so that it connects us from the previous lecture to also lecture treat. There is also going to be some homework for you to do about getting prep for homework treat that I'm going to tell you a little bit in the middle of the lecture. So today I'm going to start by, again, going through multiple examples. And I'm going to first start off by just reviewing what be it seen before. specifically kind of reviewing what the last piece that I quickly went over in the previous lecture, that was about evaluation. So let's take a look at those pieces together. So remember when I was Proposing to you the framework for creating a model, I showed you different four examples or four steps that we would go through in order to do modeling. children.title: Simple Linear Regression and Loss Functions
children.summary: The segment covers key concepts in simple linear regression, focusing on model fit through loss functions such as L1, L2, and squared loss. It highlights the estimation of optimal parameters based on an objective function and the evaluation of model performance prior to deployment. A recap of simple linear regression is provided, detailing the Y-intercept (Keta zero) and the coefficient for input, leading to predictions (Y-hat) distinct from actual values (Y). The discussion includes L2 loss, which penalizes misestimations via squared residuals, and introduces mean squared error (MSE) as the average of these squared residuals. The importance of defining the loss function, particularly the squared loss function and MSE, is emphasized in the context of empirical risk and model risk. Additionally, the parameterization of the model using TETA is discussed, noting how changes in parameters influence predictions and residuals, with TETA representing a vector of parameters that expands as more features are included.

children.title: Summary of Simple Linear Regression Concepts
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of measuring model fit using loss functions, including L1, L2, and squared loss. It emphasizes the process of estimating optimal parameters for the model based on an objective function and the necessity of evaluating the model's performance before deployment. A recap of simple linear regression is provided, highlighting the Y-intercept (referred to as Keta zero) and the coefficient applied to the input, leading to the prediction denoted as Y-hat, which differentiates it from the actual Y value.

children.title: Understanding L2 Loss and Mean Squared Error
children.summary: The discussion focuses on L2 loss, emphasizing its role in penalizing misestimations for individual data points through squared residuals. The concept of average error across the dataset is introduced, leading to the calculation of mean squared error (MSE). The process involves calculating residuals between ground truth values and predicted values, squaring these residuals, and then averaging them over the dataset. This average is defined as the mean squared error, denoted as MSE.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: We also talked about loss function, specifically what I showed you and I drive the optimum set of parameters was using the L2 loss. When I talk about L2 loss or L1 loss, that means that I'm just looking at how I can penalize the misestimations or mis-predictions for one single data point. For one single data point, L2 loss, when you're passing me the ground truth value and you're passing me Also, the predicted value is going to be this squared residual value. So that's why it's called a squared loss or L2 loss or second order loss that we're always going to use. What we talked about last time was that we don't care about individual single point in our data set, but we instead care about the average error that we're incurring over the entire data set. So that's why we would go ahead and plug in what you're seeing from why had here, we're plugging in the equivalent for y-hack, which is the equation that's going to give you the y-hack, so tetasere plus theta one times x. Then we would say that for all the ground-trust values, we would go ahead and calculate the residual between the ground-trust value of y-i, and then the residual value that we have that is calculated based on x-i. There should be a subscript x-i here. Then we're calculating all of those residuals. We're assquaring those residuals that your stingers, the square here. So it squares of the errors and then we're calculating the mean, which is calculating one over n times the summation of all of those as squares of the residuals. What we calculated last time when we wrote this function, we called this mean a squared error or m at c, which is what it is. So just the spelling out that there's mean a squared squaring the errors or residuals, that's exactly what we're trying to do. Video: 1: lec11.ipynb - JupyterLab

children.title: Empirical Risk and Objective Function in Modeling
children.summary: The function referred to as empirical risk, objective function, or model risk is denoted by R, representing the error or average error measured over the entire dataset. This segment emphasizes the importance of defining the loss function in the modeling process, specifically mentioning the squared loss function and Mean Squared Error (MSE) in the context of Simple Linear Regression.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we're calling this function, we call it empirical risk, we call it objective function, we call it model risk and different things or different ways that we're referring to this function. We are showing it by R and this R shows that this is like the error or the average error, whatever however we're measuring that error over the entire data set. Video: 1: v

children.title: Parameterization in Simple Linear Regression
children.summary: The function in simple linear regression is parametrized by TETA, which represents a family of functions determined by the values of TETA 0 and TETA 1. Changes in these parameters affect predictions, residuals, and model risk. TETA is a vector including both parameters, and as more features are added, more parameters will be included in this vector. The optimum parameters, denoted as TETA 0 hat and TETA 1 hat, were estimated using calculus to minimize risk, resulting in formulas for TETA 0 hat as y bar minus the slope times x bar, and TETA 1 hat as correlation times the standard deviation of y divided by the standard deviation of x. Additionally, visualization and evaluation methods such as root-mean-square error were briefly discussed.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And what we talked about last time was that this function is parametrized by TETA. What do I mean by parametrized by TETA? I mean that the SLR function that we had at the top is just the family of functions, right? It's just the template for all of the lines that we can have because depending on the TETA 0 and TETA 1 value, I can have different lines. And therefore, because this TETA value can change and because this prediction right here can change, Therefore, the residual can change and therefore, the value of my error or my model risk can also change. So that's why this is a function of PETA. And in this case, PETA is a vector that is including both PETA 0 and PETA 1 as well. So you will see in the next lecture and also at the end of today's lecture that if I keep adding features, I'll keep adding parameters to this vector. That's why everything is shown by just PETA, the annotation or the notation that we're using here just that it includes all the parameters that the model has. In this case, in simple linear regression, it's just two. There is TET-0 and TET-1. We also talked about at the end that we need to actually go ahead and estimate the optimum set of parameters. We did the calculus, we went ahead and calculated the partial derivative of the risk with respect to Teta 0 and also with respect to Teta 1. Two equations, two unknowns, we solved it and we arrived at the fact that Teta 0 hat and Teta 1 hat hat on top of the parameters means that that's the estimated parameter meaning that that's the optimum set of parameters that we can have is equal to exactly what you learned before in data aid, which is teta zero hat was equal to y bar minus, the slope times x bar and also the slope itself or teta one hat is equal to correlation times the standard division of y over standard division of x. That's the summary of last time. And we also talk briefly about visualization and evaluating and root-minus square error that we need to calculate. Video: 1: v

children.title: Understanding Cost Function and Residuals
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the cost function as a parameter of theta in the context of linear regression. Two plots illustrate the difference between an optimal line with minimal residuals and a suboptimal line with large residuals. The suboptimal example results in higher risk due to larger average squared errors. The goal is to determine the optimal parameters that minimize the mean squared error (MSE) across all data points, ensuring the model fits as accurately as possible.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: and I'm going to briefly talk about that in the next few slides. So kind of showing you what I meant by our function or the cost function being a parameter of theta. So what you're seeing here in two plots is that one plot is the optimum line with optimum set of parameters set to zero, hadn't had to one hat, and then the other one is a suboptimal example. In the suboptimal example, what you're seeing is that I have very large residuals. If you take a look at the residual, the mentally kind of draw all of those residual values and in your mind, the score, all of those residuals, so that it is going to penalize it even more. And average over all of those scores of the residuals, you can imagine that the suboptimal line is going to have a much higher risk as opposed to the optimal line that we have. And that's exactly what we're trying to do in order to find out the tetaside and tetaside to one value that is the optimum that passes through all of the lines as perfectly as possible and perfect means that we want to get the minimum scored error on average on all of the data points that we have. children.title: Estimation vs. Production in Modeling
children.summary: The concepts of estimation and production are clarified, where production refers to obtaining outputs from a model regardless of its optimality, termed as a prediction task. Estimation involves calculating model parameters based on data, exemplified by simple linear regression (SLR) to find parameters like the intercept and slope. The reliability of this estimation depends on the dataset, and methods to enhance accuracy relative to the target population are discussed. The process of minimizing mean squared error (MSE) during SLR is identified as least squares estimation, which involves estimating parameters by minimizing squared residuals.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Kind of reminding you of the terminology that I've been carefully using in the past lecture and also in today's lecture. There is a notion of estimation and there is a notion of production. When I talk about production, what do I mean by production? I mean that I have an input and I just want to plug into whatever model it is, regardless of whether it's optimal or non-optimal, and I want to get production. The production can be good, the production can be bad, So irrespective of that, any time that you're getting a prediction from any model, that's called a prediction task. When I go ahead and go through the process of finding the optimum set of parameters for the model using the data, so it's called using the data calculating model parameters. This is called estimation. So for example, when I have this simple linear regression, I would say that I go through the estimation process in order to find out that the zero hadn't, that the one had. And I call it estimation because we are basically estimating those parameters based on the data set that we have. And we're going to talk about how reliable this estimation is, depending on your data set and how you can make sure that your estimation is as accurately as possible compared to the population of target that you have in mind. When we are doing the SLR and optimizing SLR or every time that we are trying to minimize mean scored error, we call that we are doing least the squares estimation. Because we are just estimating some parameters for some model. using the least the squares lost function. So when you say least the squares when you hear about least the squares regression that means that it's a regression line. Video: 1: lec11.ipynb - JupyterLab

This is called estimation. So for example, when I have this simple linear regression, I would say that I go through the estimation process in order to find out that the zero hadn't, that the one had. And I call it estimation because we are basically estimating those parameters based on the data set that we have. And we're going to talk about how reliable this estimation is, depending on your data set and how you can make sure that your estimation is as accurately as possible compared to the population of target that you have in mind. When we are doing the SLR and optimizing SLR or every time that we are trying to minimize mean scored error, we call that we are doing least the squares estimation. Because we are just estimating some parameters for some model. using the least the squares lost function. So when you say least the squares when you hear about least the squares regression that means that it's a regression line.

children.title: Terminology: Prediction vs. Estimation in Modeling
children.summary: Prediction and estimation are terms often used interchangeably, but they have distinct meanings. Estimation involves using data to calculate model parameters, while prediction refers to using a model to forecast outputs for unseen data. Parameters are estimated by minimizing average loss, specifically through Least Squares Estimation, which selects parameters that minimize Mean Squared Error (MSE).

children.title: Estimation vs. Prediction and Evaluation Importance
children.summary: The segment discusses the distinction between estimation and prediction, emphasizing that once optimal parameters are known, predictions can be made by plugging in x values. It highlights the importance of evaluation before conducting regression analysis, including calculating statistics such as mean, standard deviation, and correlation to ensure a linear association. If correlation is sufficiently high, modeling can proceed. The concept of root-mean-square error (RMSE) is explained, noting that it provides error reporting in the same units as the target variable, making it easier to interpret and compare models.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So once I have the optimal parameters or irrespective of whether I have optimal parameters or not, if every time that I plug in the x value and these parameters are known, I can just go ahead and get my prediction. So that's the difference between estimation and prediction. And I just want to make sure that we're all on the same page. So again, this is kind of the summary of where we are. And the last piece that I quickly went over in the previous lecture that I'm going to go ahead and emphasize one more time was about evaluation. Why is evaluation important? It's important because we don't want to blindly go ahead and just run regression analysis on any data set that we have. First, we need to calculate some statistics. We need to make sure that we know the meanest standard deviation correlation more importantly to just make sure that there is linear association before doing any kind of regression analysis, right? And if the correlation was high enough, then we would go ahead and do the modeling. We talked about root-missgr error. So root-missgr error means that I'm calculating the same MSE function that I was trying to optimize. But this time, I'm going to tell you the square root of this MSE function, because what I want to do is I want to make sure that I'm reporting the error in the same unit as y. So why is that the case? For example, say that I'm predicting some length value, length is the target that I'm predicting. If I tell you the error in, for example, meter is score, it's hard for you to make sense of what that meter is score is and how do you compare different models. As opposed to me going back to the meter kind of unit and telling you that, okay, the error on average is two meters, are you fine with it or not? Video: 1: lec11.ipynb - JupyterLab

children.title: Evaluating Model Performance and Residuals
children.summary: Model evaluation involves comparing different models to assess their performance. A key aspect is visualizing residuals, calculated as the difference between the ground-truth value and the predicted value. Residuals can be positive or negative, and plotting them should reveal no discernible pattern. A patterned residual plot indicates that the model has not captured all trends in the data, suggesting the need for further transformations to ensure that remaining residuals represent only measurement noise. Additionally, model evaluation includes visualizing data, computing statistics like means and standard deviations, and using performance metrics such as Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) to quantify model accuracy.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then you would with this type whether the model is accurate enough or not. It's just kind of a way of comparing different models and evaluating the performance of the model once we're done with the modeling piece. And then the last piece that I, again, mentioned was that we always want to visualize residuals. Remember, residual was calculated using this equation that we said that it's equal to the ground-trose value minus the predicted value. So there is sometimes that the residual is positive, sometimes that the residual is negative. And what we learned from data aid is that if we plot the residual plot, we do not want to see any pattern. The reason that we don't want to see any pattern is because if you see a pattern in the residual, that means that there was some pattern in the underlying data that you did the model. So your model was not capturing the entire trend or the entire characteristic in the data. So you have to go back. Do any kind of transformation that we'll see at the end of today's lecture in order to make sure that you literally model everything that's possible in the data so that the remaining residual is basically noise in measurement kind of. And that's why we want to make sure that the residual plot is kind of uniformly distributed across X and also across Y and there is no. kind of pattern that we can see in the output. children.title: Analyzing Anzcom Cortet Dataset Characteristics
children.summary: The segment discusses the Anzcom Cortet dataset, which includes four distinct datasets with the same mean, standard deviation, and correlations, yet they produce different results, suggesting potential inaccuracies in model predictions. It emphasizes the importance of visualizing data before analysis to uncover probabilistic characteristics. Additionally, methods for evaluating model fit are introduced, including data visualization, computing statistics like means and standard deviations, and performance metrics such as Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), where a lower RMSE indicates better model performance.

children.title: Analyzing Anzcom Cortet Dataset Characteristics
children.summary: The segment discusses the Anzcom Cortet dataset, which consists of four distinct datasets that share the same mean, standard deviation, and correlations. Despite these similarities, the datasets yield different results, indicating that the model predictions may not be accurate. The importance of visualizing data prior to analysis is emphasized to identify probabilistic characteristics. Additionally, methods for evaluating model fit are introduced, including data visualization, computing statistics such as means and standard deviations, and performance metrics like Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), which measures prediction accuracy. A lower RMSE indicates better model performance.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I'm going to start by talking about a specific dataset. I'm going to show you the demo. This is specific dataset is called Anzcom Cortet. There is four datasets in this specific dataset. And we're going to take a look at the characteristics of all of these four datasets. So with all of these four datasets that we have, The mean is standard, the average of x, average of y, a standard deviation for x and y, and the correlations are exactly the same, but the data sets are different. So we're going to take a look at what the data set is exactly. And then because our standard deviation mean, and all of those things are the same, therefore the tetasero hat and tetah1 hat is going to be the same, meaning that same line is supposed to pass through and model all four of these data sets. But what we'll see is that it's not accurate. And we could have told, we could have kind of identified that it's probabilistic if we have visualized the data ahead of time. So I'm going to go to the demo actually and I have a... I'm gonna restart my carnal. and i'm going to Here are all the outputs. Video: 1: lec11.ipynb - JupyterLab

children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of various functions necessary for data analysis, including calculating standard units, correlation, slope, intercept, and fitting a least squares line. It also mentions the evaluation of the line through mean squared error. Additionally, it introduces a dataset comprising four datasets where X is shared among the first three datasets (X, Y1; X, Y2; X, Y3) while the last dataset (X4, Y4) has a different X and Y. Visualizations are briefly mentioned, but the details of the code for plotting are not elaborated.

We will first define some helper functions to calculate the regression line for us.
[3]:
# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

We will first define some helper functions to calculate the regression line for us.
[3]:
# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)| I
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

# Big font helper
def adjust_fontsize(size=None):
SMALL_SIZE = 8
MEDIUM SIZE = 10
BIGGER_SIZE = 12
if size != None:
SMALL_SIZE = MEDIUM SIZE = BIGGER_SIZE = size
plt.rc('font', size=SMALL_SIZE)
controls default text sizes
plt.rc('axes', titlesize=SMALL_SIZE)
# fontsize of the axes title
plt.rc('axes', labelsize=MEDIUM_SIZE)
# fontsize of the x and y labels
plt.rc('xtick', labelsize=SMALL_SIZE)
# fontsize of the tick labels

We will first define some helper functions to calculate the regression line for us.
[3]:
# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

# Helper functions
def standard_units(x):
return (x - np.mean(x)) / np.std(x)
def correlation(x, y):
return np.mean(standard_units(x) * standard_units(y))
def slope(x, y):
return correlation(x, y) * np.std(y) / np.std(x)
def intercept(x, y):
return np.mean(y) - slope(x, y)*np.mean(x)
def fit_least_squares(x, y):
theta_0 = intercept(x, y)
theta_1 = slope(x, y)
return theta_0, theta_1
def predict(x, theta_0, theta_1):
return theta_0 + theta_1*x
def compute_mse(y, yhat) :
return np.mean((y - yhat) ** 2)

elif visualize == RESID:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(4, 3))
ax_resid = plt.gca()
if ax_resid is not None:
ax_resid.scatter(x, y - yhat, color = 'red' )
ax_resid.plot([4, 14], [0, 0], color = 'black')
ax_resid.set_title("Residuals")

print(f"r = Correlation(x, y): {correlation(x, y) :. 3f}")
# Performance metrics
ahat, bhat = fit_least_squares(x, y)
yhat = predict(x, ahat, bhat)
print(f"\theta_0: {ahat: . 2f}, \theta_1: {bhat :. 2f}")
print(f"RMSE: {np.sqrt(compute_mse(y, yhat)) :. 3f}")
# visualization
fig, ax_resid = None, None
if visualize == RESID_SCATTER:
fig, axs = plt.subplots(1,2, figsize=(8, 3))
axs[0].scatter(x, y)
axs[0].plot(x, yhat)
axs[0].set_title("LS fit")
ax_resid = axs [1]
elif visualize == RESID:
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(4, 3))
ax_resid = plt.gca()
if ax_resid is not None:
ax_resid.scatter(x, y - yhat, color = 'red' )
ax_resid.plot([4, 14], [0, 0], color = 'black')
ax_resid.set_title("Residuals")
return fig

children.title: Analysis of Theta Values in Datasets
children.summary: Calculating the theta_0 and theta_1 values for four different datasets shows that these values are identical across all datasets. This consistency is confirmed through the mean, standard deviation, and correlation of x and y, which are the same for each dataset. A demonstration of fitting a simple linear regression model to the datasets is included, emphasizing the statistical properties that result in these consistent theta values.

children.title: Data Visualization and Correlation Analysis Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of visualizing datasets before modeling, presenting four datasets to illustrate different correlation characteristics. Dataset one exhibits a strong positive linear trend with a correlation of 0.8, while dataset two shows a low correlation of 0.1 with no linear trend, suggesting a higher-order relationship. Dataset three has a linear trend but includes an outlier, and dataset four is represented as a vertical line with an outlier as well. The speaker stresses the importance of visual inspection to identify nonlinear trends and outliers that need to be addressed prior to statistical modeling.

children.title: Data Visualization and Correlation Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the importance of visualizing datasets before modeling. It presents four datasets, highlighting their correlations: dataset one shows a strong positive linear trend (correlation of 0.8), while dataset two has a low correlation (0.1) with no linear trend, indicating a higher-order relationship. Dataset three presents a linear trend but contains an outlier, while dataset four is a vertical line with an outlier as well. The speaker emphasizes that visual inspection is crucial to identify nonlinear trends and outliers that should be addressed prior to statistical modeling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what I'm going to do is I'm going to actually go ahead and try to plot these data sets so you can kind of compare that. So I'm going to zoom out slightly. I can zoom out, but this is data set one. So you can see that, okay, there was a correlation of point eight. So it's kind of a linear positive trend between X and Y. That's fine. For this one, the correlation says that is point one, but you're seeing that there is no linear trend, but it's a higher order relationship between x and y. But this one is a linear trend, but there is an outlying outlier sitting right here. And with this one, it's a line, but it's just the vertical line, and there is also an outlier, that's x4 or data set 4 that we had in the data set. So if I go ahead and just plot the line that we got, which basically had the same tets of zero and tets of 1 value for all of these four data sets, what we will get is actually all of these lines. So you see there is this line for data set one, this line for data set two, for data set three and data set four. So this is kind of the case that I went ahead and calculated the statistics. Correlation was high enough, I went ahead and did the modeling. But I should have visualized the data set beforehand so that I could see visually that there is nonlinear trend or there is outlier that I have to remove before doing the modeling. leaf_chunk.audio: So what I'm going to do is I'm going to actually go ahead and try to plot these data sets so you can kind of compare that. So I'm going to zoom out slightly. I can zoom out, but this is data set one. So you can see that, okay, there was a correlation of point eight. So it's kind of a linear positive trend between X and Y. That's fine. For this one, the correlation says that is point one, but you're seeing that there is no linear trend, but it's a higher order relationship between x and y. But this one is a linear trend, but there is an outlying outlier sitting right here. And with this one, it's a line, but it's just the vertical line, and there is also an outlier, that's x4 or data set 4 that we had in the data set. So if I go ahead and just plot the line that we got, which basically had the same tets of zero and tets of 1 value for all of these four data sets, what we will get is actually all of these lines. So you see there is this line for data set one, this line for data set two, for data set three and data set four. So this is kind of the case that I went ahead and calculated the statistics. Correlation was high enough, I went ahead and did the modeling. But I should have visualized the data set beforehand so that I could see visually that there is nonlinear trend or there is outlier that I have to remove before doing the modeling.

children.title: Understanding and Analyzing Residual Plots
children.summary: The discussion covers residual plots, which represent the difference between actual values (y) and predicted values (y hat). A residual of zero indicates perfect prediction, with points above the line showing underestimation and points below indicating overestimation. It's crucial to have an equal distribution of residuals above and below the line, aligning with the idea that average residuals should equal zero. Residual plots help identify unaccounted patterns in data; a good plot shows no discernible pattern, while patterns like vertical lines indicate the need for additional feature engineering. This involves calculating statistics, visualizing data, and analyzing residuals to ensure all trends are captured before returning to modeling.

children.title: Understanding Residual Plots and Predictions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on residual plots, explaining that a residual plot indicates the difference between actual values (y) and predicted values (y hat). A residual of zero means perfect prediction, represented by a line where points above indicate underestimation and points below indicate overestimation. It emphasizes the importance of having an equal distribution of residuals above and below the line, as well as across the x-axis, aligning with the concept that the average residuals should equal zero, ensuring an equal number of over and under estimations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And because I've not done that, then the residual plots are kind of looking funny. So for this case, the residual plot is fine. So this is the residual plot that we're plotting, which is equal to residual being equal to 0, which basically means that y minus y hat is equal to 0, which effectively means that y is equal to y hat. So that's the line of perfect prediction. So if the points are on that line, that's perfect prediction. If the points are above that line, that means that there was under estimation and then over estimation is under the line. So that's kind of how you can see. Because we are we know from the previous lecture that with msc the average errors or the average residuals is equal to equal to zero. We should have equal number of over estimations and under estimations and so on and so forth. That's why I really want to have equal distribution above the line and equal distribution below the line and also the same distribution across the x-axis. And we're going to see an example of that 11 later. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Analyzing Residual Plots and Feature Engineering
children.summary: Residual plots are used to identify patterns in data that have not been captured by the model. A good residual plot shows no pattern, indicating that the model has captured the underlying trends effectively. If patterns are present, such as vertical lines or uneven distribution, it suggests that additional feature engineering may be necessary. The process involves calculating statistics, visualizing data, and analyzing residuals to ensure all trends are accounted for before moving back to the modeling phase. Upcoming segments will include practical examples, though they may focus more on derivations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is kind of a reasonably good residual plot doesn't show any pattern, but if I go ahead and scroll down and I show the residual plot for this one, this definitely shows the pattern, which basically tells me that there was some underlying pattern in the data that that I haven't captured. Maybe I should go back and do more feature in generating, and we actually have a feature engineering lecture later on in the data 100. So that I captured the entire trend in the data so that whatever is left is kind of just noise here and not a pattern, right? That's why we don't want residual plot to have any pattern. For this one, there is still this kind of line that I'm seeing as a pattern, which is not good. For this one, the same thing, the residuals are not equally distributed across the x-axis. And that's why kind of not the x-axis, but it's basically also showing that same pattern that there's vertical line, vertical line pattern for data set for it that we want. So. That's exactly the reason that we go through all of these processes. We calculate the statistics, we visualize beforehand, we calculate rudeness, squared errors, and then after we're done, we will actually go ahead and take a look at the residual plots just to make sure that is there any additional pattern that you should have extracted that you did not so that you go back to the EDAPR, do more feature engineering, and then come back to the modeling phase of your data science life cycle. So with that out of the way, I want to jump into two examples. So the next part, the next two parts iteration to an iteration three are going to be kind of easy. They're going to be just more practice, but they're not going to be as fun because I'm going to still do all of those derivations that are going to be a little bit not so interesting. children.title: Introduction to Constant Model in Regression
children.summary: The focus is on training data scientists to understand the mechanics behind algorithms rather than just using machine learning frameworks. A constant model is introduced as a simpler approach compared to simple linear regression. An example involving sales predictions at a Boba shop illustrates how past sales data can be used to estimate future sales, leading into a discussion on determining the optimum constant model.

children.title: Introduction to Constant Model in Regression
children.summary: The focus is on training data scientists to understand the mechanics behind algorithms rather than just using machine learning frameworks. A constant model will be introduced as a simpler approach compared to simple linear regression. An example involving sales predictions at a Boba shop is presented, where past sales data is used to estimate future sales, leading into a discussion on how to determine the optimum constant model.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But we'll get comfortable with it because you have to get comfortable with these things as data scientists. What we want to train at Berkeley is data scientists who actually know all the mechanics and also all the cores of all the algorithms as opposed to just using the black boxes from all of the these machine learning frameworks, right? And you're going to gain that by going through all of these math heavy lectures and you will appreciate it over time because you will understand, again, all the details and core part of the MLR algorithms moving forward. So Um. I want to move on to a simpler model, believe it or not, we're going to move back instead of doing simple linear regression, I want to have a constant model. So that's the model that I'll choose and then I'm going to keep everything else the same and I'm going to go over like how I can drive what is the optimum constant model that we can have. But let me tell you what the constant model is. And I'm going to tell you with an example. Let's assume that you work at a Boba shop. There is a good Boba shop by Soda that I like. And you recorded the number of Boba teas that you sold over the past five days. And I come in and I tell you that how many Boba teas do you think you will sell tomorrow? So the number of bobbets is that you sell before is 2021-2229, EnterD3, and the options are 0-25-2200 and something else. So let's see what you think. This is the first slide up. children.title: Objective Functions and Model Estimation Techniques
children.summary: The segment covers the definition and significance of objective functions in estimation models, particularly the constant model, which simplifies predictions by using past sales data without daily variations. An example of boba tea sales illustrates the fixed value approach, where the constant model outputs a single value, theta 0, and is simpler than linear regression with only one parameter. The optimal theta 0 hat is derived using mean squared error (MSE) as the loss function, involving averaging squared loss values and optimizing via derivatives. It emphasizes the importance of parameters like theta 0 hat in machine learning for effective predictions, suggesting a review of calculus and linear algebra concepts. The discussion also includes minimizing the objective function to find theta zero hat at y bar, highlighting its role in determining sample variance and the mean as a key statistic, along with comparing constant models to linear regression in the estimation process.

children.title: Objective Functions and Constant Models in Estimation
children.summary: The segment discusses the significance of defining an objective function in estimation models, particularly focusing on the constant model, which simplifies predictions by ignoring daily variations and relying solely on past sales data. An example using boba tea sales illustrates this model's fixed value approach. It explains that the constant model outputs a single value, theta 0, and is simpler than linear regression, having only one parameter. The optimal theta 0 hat is derived by calculating the mean squared error (MSE) as the loss function, which involves averaging the squared loss values. The optimization process includes taking derivatives of the loss function to find the optimal value for theta 0, specifically in relation to the boba tea dataset.

children.title: Objective Functions and Constant Models in Estimation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the importance of defining an objective function in estimation models, particularly in the context of a constant model. It highlights how different objective functions can lead to varying outcomes and optimum values based on the penalization of estimation errors. The constant model, also referred to as a summary statistic, makes simplifying assumptions, such as disregarding daily variations like temperature or specific events, leading to consistent predictions based solely on past sales data. An example involving boba tea sales illustrates the model's reliance on fixed values without incorporating new input data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Because I haven't told you what should be your objective function and I haven't told you what day tomorrow is right so you're missing that input information. But let alone if you're doing the constant model depending on how I want to penalize your estimation your objective function is going to be different right. And we're going to take a look at how different objective functions might lead to different outcomes or different optimum value that you can. So this was a constant model. And I tell you that how many bobbosies you will sell tomorrow. And I come back the day after and I tell how many bobbosies you will sell tomorrow and tomorrow. Effectively, you have to have a way of measuring how good your estimation should be and how to penalize your own miscalculations or mis-predictions. And you will keep telling me the same value. You will not change your information or you change your model because there is no input to your data. And you only have those five days. and you will keep giving me the same constant value. So a constant model is a model that is also called summary statistic. It makes a lot of simplifying assumption. Simplifying assumption, for example, in the boba tea example is that it assumes nothing about the day. It assumes nothing about the temperature. It assumes nothing about, for example, whether tomorrow is Calday or not. So it basically says that no information in, I'm just going to do production. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Understanding the Constant Model in Regression
children.summary: The constant model, a simplified predictive model, always outputs the same value, theta 0, which is a single constant rather than a variable dependent on inputs. This model is simpler than simple linear regression as it has only one parameter, making it easier to visualize and understand different loss functions. The focus is on deriving the optimal theta 0 hat for the model, starting with the objective function, while maintaining the mean squared error as the loss function.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So it makes a lot of simplifying assumptions. And because of that, every time that you get, if you want to do prediction, it's just going to give you theta 0. Teta 0 is going to be the output always. And that's the constant value. That's why it's called a constant model. As you can tell, it's much simpler than simple linear regression, because there is no input. There is no theta 1 parameter. There's only one parameter that you're going to deal with. And the reason that I picked this model because it's going to be simpler for you to visualize and make sense of different loss functions, playing with this model, and then kind of seeing the impact of them side by side. Again, this constant model always gives me think about it as a machine. It was also a machine when we were looking at simple linear regression, but this time the machine is all only parametrized by tetasera. Every time that you want to get inference, I will just tell you tetasera as the production and the tetasera value is just the real value number. It's not a vector. It's just a real single value number that you will get as the predicted value. So The model is fixed as a constant model. The last function, I'm assuming that I'm going to stick with the same mean scored error and then the fun part begins. I want to do the calculus and then go through and derive and find the actual theta, theta zero hat that is optimum for the model that we had at hand. So with the example that we had in mind with or generally with any data set, I first need to go ahead and write my entire objective function. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

The Constant Model
ab
T
The constant model, also known as a summary statistic, summarizes the data by always
1078489
"predicting" the same number-i.e ., predicting a constant.
It ignores any relationships between variables.
For instance, boba tea sales likely depend on the time of year, the weather, how the
customers feel, whether school is in session, etc.
Ignoring these factors is a simplifying assumption.
The constant model is also a parametric, statistical model:
y = 00
Our parameter 00 is 1-dimensional 00 E IR
00
12.
We now have no input into our model; we predict @ = 00.
Like before, we can still determine the best 00 that
minimizes average loss on our data.

children.title: Optimizing Mean Squared Error with Theta
children.summary: The objective function involves calculating the squirt loss values for all data points and averaging them. The parameterized loss function depends on the value of theta (TETA), and the goal is to find the optimal theta 0 that minimizes the cost function. The Mean Squared Error (MSE) is defined as the average squared loss over the dataset, represented mathematically. The function simplifies to depend only on theta 0, allowing for optimization using direct derivatives.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: The objective function was always that if you're thinking that you're going to use a squirt loss, you have to go ahead and calculate those squirt loss values for all of the data points and then go ahead and calculate the average over all of them. And again, you know that I've repeated it multiple times that is parametrized by TETA, depending on teta 0 value that you pick, you'll get a different loss value. And our goal is to find out what is the optimum teta 0 that gives us the minimum cost function for the cost value for this objective function. What I've done here is that I replaced yi had with teta 0 because that makes it simpler. So my function is now a very simple function that the y i's are always given from the data set and the only other argument that I have or the parameter that I have is theta 0. And I just need to optimize this function that is parametrized by theta 0, with respect to theta 0, which means that I can just use direct derivatives. So writing it again. There are different approaches that I can do it. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Calculus for Optimization in Boba Tea Dataset
children.summary: The process of finding the optimum theta zero hat value involves taking the derivative of the loss function, which represents the average of the squared errors. While for small datasets, such as the boba tea example, direct calculation is feasible, larger datasets with millions of data points make this impractical. An algebraic trick exists to simplify the process, but the focus remains on deriving the objective function and setting the derivative with respect to theta zero to zero to solve for theta zero hat.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: There is one way that I can just go through calculus, just take the derivative, find the optimum point and the minimum point, and then report that minimum point as the optimum theta zero hat value. With the dataset of boba t, because it was a small enough, you could just plug into values and just find the optimum value. It's just the normal calculation that you would do. But usually we don't have the luxury of having This is small data set and we would usually have millions and millions of data points. So that's why calculating directly is not feasible. For this is specific case, there's also an algebraic trick that I'm not gonna cover, because I'm not interested in those tricks, but there is few slides at the end of the lecture slides if you wanna go over and then see how you could have avoided calculus. Hopefully you will not want to avoid calculus moving on, but if you want to do that, there's an algebraic trick, specifically for this kind of model because it's kind of a simple model, but only one variable. children.title: Differentiating Summations in One-Dimensional Functions
children.summary: The discussion focuses on differentiating a one-dimensional function with respect to a single parameter, emphasizing that the derivative of a summation can be expressed as a sum of derivatives. The first step in this process involves recognizing that the objective function is a summation of errors across the dataset, which allows for the application of this differentiation rule.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's kind of the framework that we started using in a previous lecture. And that's what we'll continue doing. But this time it's going to be simpler. Notice that instead of partial derivative, I have derivative because I only have one argument or one parameter. And technically it's a one-dimensional function that I can just directly take derivative up. So when I take the derivative with respect to tet 0, I notice that there is only this single term. So we'll keep thinking about how I can... Take the derivative of that single term that is hidden between multiple summations and also it is a score as well. Remember from last time that the first step always is going to be that I can turn the derivative of a summation into a sum of derivatives, right? Because you will always be doing the derivative of a summation because always your objective function is some summation of the error over the entire data set. So that's always going to be your first step. So I'm going to do that. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Derivative of Loss Function with Chain Rule
children.summary: The segment discusses taking the derivative of the loss function, specifically the squared difference between observed values (yi) and the parameter (theta0). The coefficient 1/n is factored out, and the chain rule is applied to differentiate the function f, defined as (yi - theta0). The derivative results in a simplification involving negative constants, leading to the expression -2/n times the summation of (yi - theta0) over i from 1 to n.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I'm going to say that I'm taking out the coefficient 1 over n. I'm taking out the summation from i, i from 1 up to n. And then I have the derivative of yi minus theta 0 square with respect to theta 0. Okay. So I'm going to show you one thing that I also showed you last time that if you think about why i minus k to 0 as function f, I would call it What I'm doing here is that I want to now take the derivative of this function f to the power of 2 with respect to theta 0. And I can use the chain rule in order to take this derivative. This is equal to two times function f times the derivative of function f with respect to theta zero. Right? And I'm just going to go ahead and write this out and apply this chain rule. So effectively this is going to be this coefficient that I had one over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of 2 times f, what was f? Why i minus? Head to zero. is the derivative of effort respect to theta zero? It is negative one because the only term that I have... Here is head to zero that I would take the derivative that is non-zero and then the derivative of negative T0 with respect to T0 is just that coefficient of negative 1. So I can just directly put that in. And then I'll just go ahead and simplify all of these constants negative 2 over n of the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus T0. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Differentiation and Setting Derivatives to Zero
children.summary: The segment emphasizes the importance of practicing derivatives, particularly in the context of homework five, which provides ample opportunity for practice. It discusses the process of differentiation with respect to a variable, solving for that variable, and highlights the derivative of a sum being equal to the sum of the derivatives. Additionally, it mentions the application of the chain rule and the necessity of setting the derived equation equal to zero to find critical points.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay. So so far simple. I'll keep doing it. I'll keep taking derivatives and you'll get comfortable with it. If I can do it in front of the class, you can just do it in the homeworks and you will actually do it in your homework five. It's a very fun homework. and you will get a lot of practice with it, but you will have to start early. Setting it equal to zero. When I set it equal to zero. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Finding Optimum: Derivative Equals Zero
children.summary: The process of finding the optimum involves setting the derivative equal to zero. The equation derived is -2/n times the summation from i=1 to n of (yi - theta_0 hat) = 0. By manipulating the equation, it is shown that the summation of yi equals n times theta_0 hat, indicating a relationship between the unknowns and the constant theta_0 hat.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What happens? So I'm finding the optimum. When optimum happens when the derivative is equal to zero, which means that instead of ket to zero, now I have ket to zero half. So I'm going to write this as negative 2 over n of the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus theta 0 hat this time equal to 0. and solve it for 10 to zero hat, which again is going to be... that negative 2 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus theta 0 hat is equal to 0. I can just multiply 2 sides by negative n over 2 to get rid of all of those coefficients over there, which effectively means that I can cross this out. and I'll write this as summation of i from 1 up to n of yi minus set to 0 hat equal to 0. I'll separate those two terms. I'll turn this into i from 1 up to n of yi minus... The summation of i from one up to n of teta zero had equal to zero. I can take this teta 0 hat summation to the other side so that I can get rid of the negative sign and then I will have one term related to teta 0 hat which respect to something else that means that I have solved it. So this effectively means that I have the summation of i from 1 up to n of yi, which is equal to summation of i from 1 up to n of teta 0 hat. Which the summation of i from 1 up to n of t to 0 hat, which is a constant, is going to be equal to n times t to 0 hat. Right? So then everything is going to be simple. I have one side that I have theta zero hat and a coefficient and I have this other side that I have a summation of the things that are unknown to me. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Calculating Theta Zero Hat and Derivatives
children.summary: Theta zero hat is calculated as the average of the y values, represented as the summation of y_i divided by n. This approach aligns with the intuition that a constant prediction should be the average of known values. The segment also covers the differentiation process for the general case, including setting up the equation R(theta) = summation of (y_i - theta)^2, differentiating with respect to theta, and simplifying the expression to find the optimal theta that minimizes the error.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I have all of those inputs and I have all of those y i values. So I can directly go ahead and say that the theta zero hat is equal to the summation of i from one up to n of y i divided by n, which we know is equal to average of y or y bar. why a lot of you picked 25 because that was your initial intuition that you have to take average and if you want to give a constant prediction, it's better that that constant prediction be the average of all the values that you already know. And you're going to see another way that it can also make sense in another way and get a different value. How are we doing? Good. Sideways. Bad no idea what's going on. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Importance of Parameters in Machine Learning Models
children.summary: The segment emphasizes the necessity of understanding and utilizing parameters, particularly theta 0 hat, in machine learning. It highlights that while the actual value of the cost function is less significant, the parameter that minimizes this cost is crucial for making predictions and building models. Additionally, it stresses the importance of foundational concepts in calculus and linear algebra for effective model development, suggesting that students may need to review lectures multiple times to fully grasp these ideas.

children.title: Importance of Parameters in Machine Learning Models
children.summary: The segment emphasizes the necessity of understanding and utilizing parameters, specifically theta 0 hat, in machine learning. It notes that while the actual value of the cost function is less significant, the parameter that minimizes this cost is crucial for making predictions and building models. The discussion highlights the importance of grasping foundational concepts in calculus and linear algebra for effective model development, suggesting that students may need to review lectures multiple times to fully comprehend these ideas.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, that will make sense. To tell you the truth, these three lectures, you have to watch them at least twice. Maybe the next lecture you'll have to watch it three times. So that's kind of the trend. But once you build these foundations, you will really be proud of yourself because you will make use of all of these calculus that you've learned. And actually, next lecture, you will actually use some linear algebra in order to build ML models, which is really interesting. Okay, moving on. So this is what we found out and kind of summarizing everything that we had seen so far. We had seen that Y bar is my optimum parameter or my theta 0 hat. If I go ahead and plug in Y bar into my objective function, this is going to give me this equation, which effectively is the sample variance or sigma to the power, sigma, a standard division of Y to the power of 2. What this means? Is interesting but it's not necessarily useful what I want to tell you is that in machine learning what we're really interested in is the actual why I Why bar value what is the tetra zero hats that gives me the optimum value we care really less about what that actual value is and I'm going to show you with an example. And another reason that we care about the parameter itself and not the actual value of the cost function that that parameter is giving us is that once you have the parameter you have the model you can draw it you can do prediction and what not you care less about what was the purpose. What was its performance on the original training data that you had? So that's why we care less about the value of the cost, but we care about the argument that gave us the minimum value for the cost function. And that actually has a term. So we always say that. children.title: Minimizing Objective Function and Sample Variance
children.summary: The objective is to minimize the function to find the optimal value of theta zero hat, which is achieved at y bar. This value is crucial in the model, and substituting theta zero hat into the objective function yields the sample variance. The emphasis is on identifying the argument that minimizes the function, highlighting the mean as a key summary statistic.

children.title: Minimizing Objective Function and Sample Variance
children.summary: To find the optimal value of theta zero hat, the goal is to minimize the objective function across all possible values of theta zero. The value that minimizes this function is y bar, which is significant in the context of the model. Additionally, when plugging theta zero hat into the objective function, the minimum value obtained corresponds to the sample variance. The focus is on the argument that minimizes the function rather than the minimum value itself, reinforcing the mean's role as a common summary statistic.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What we are trying to do in order to find out the teta zero hat is that across all of the possible teta zero values that we had remember I was saying that the when I give you a model I give you a family of functions I give you all different possible values that teta zero or teta one could take so I'm saying that across all of the teta zero values find the argument that actually minimizes what minimizes the objective function And the argument or the value that minimizes the objective function in our case is exactly y bar. And that's what we care about. You could also argue that, okay, if I want to go ahead and just get this tet to zero that you had or this tet to zero hat that you had. And I plug it into this objective function that I had. What is the minimum value that I'll get at the end? the minimum value that you'll get at the end is probably, or you can calculate it that is going to be sample variance. But again, we care less about the minimum value of a function, but we care more about the argument. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Model Comparison and Cost Function Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the estimation process in modeling, comparing a constant model to a linear regression for predicting dugong ages. It evaluates the assumptions and sacrifices involved in simplifying from linear regression to a constant model, emphasizing the mean squared error (MSE) as a key summary statistic. The next step involves plotting the cost function for the constant model, represented as a function of theta, with a focus on visualizing the loss landscape by enumerating theta values from -20 to 42. Additionally, the properties of convex functions are covered, highlighting how their curvature indicates the minimum can be found where the derivative equals zero. The discussion includes building a cost function with inputs theta zero and theta one in simple linear regression, leading to a three-dimensional cost landscape.

children.title: Impact of Constant Model vs. Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment discusses the estimation process in modeling and evaluates the impact of using a constant model compared to a more complex model, specifically in predicting the age of dugongs. Two approaches are examined: one using only the ages with a constant model and another using the lengths of dugongs in a simple linear regression to predict age. The analysis includes considerations of assumptions made when simplifying from linear regression to a constant model, the potential sacrifices involved, and the overall shape of the cost function. Additionally, it highlights that the optimal parameter for the constant model relates to the mean squared error (MSE), reinforcing the mean's role as a common summary statistic.

children.title: Plotting Cost Function for Constant Model
children.summary: The data set for the constant model is available on the data hub, and the next step involves plotting the cost function for this model. The cost function, or objective function, is represented as a function of theta, with theta 0 on the x-axis and corresponding costs on the y-axis, creating a two-dimensional loss landscape. The process involves enumerating over possible values of theta 0, specifically within the range of -20 to 42, to build the mean squared error (MSE) for each value. The visualization of loss for different theta values is emphasized, highlighting the importance of visualizing datasets before fitting models.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So the data set for the constant model, data set for the linear model, the data is also. Posted on data hub so the data is loaded. So what I'm doing next is that I'm going to plot this cost function for the case that I have a constant model on this specific data set. So what you're seeing here is that the cost function or this objective function is the function of theta, right? You see that theta 0 is on the x axis for different theta 0 values. I have different cost on the y axis. This is a two-dimensional lost landscape. So this is the first time you're hearing that term. If you will keep hearing that term. on and on and every time that we're trying to optimize a model. So how did I arrive at that? Basically, I said that I'm going to just enumerate over all possible values of Tetas. And I'm assuming that I'm just going to only look at the range that I think that my Tetas 0 had is going to be in it. So I'm going to look at the data point, the Tetas 0 being from negative 20 to 42. And I'm going to go ahead and just build this MSE, built this constant model or with the with the msc objective function for that is specific teta 0 and then I will take you technically take a look at that loss and everything else is just plotting that loss for different theta values. So again you see that for different theta values if I go ahead and set teta 0 to be equal to 0 this is the amount of loss that I'll get. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

%matplotlib inline
def mse_constant(theta, data):
return np.mean(np.array([(y_obs - theta) ** 2 for y_obs in data]), axis=0)
thetas = np.linspace(-20, 42, 1000)
12_loss_thetas = mse_constant(thetas, data_constant)
plt.plot(thetas, 12_loss_thetas)
plt.xlabel(r'$\theta_0$')
plt.ylabel(r'MSE')
# Optimal point
thetahat = np.mean(data_constant)

def mse_constant(theta, data):
return np.mean(np.array([(y_obs - theta) ** 2 for y_obs in data]), axis=0)
thetas = np.linspace(-20, 42, 1000) I

children.title: Convex Functions and Cost Landscapes in Regression
children.summary: The discussion covers the properties of convex functions, specifically how their upward curvature indicates that the minimum can be found where the derivative equals zero. The focus shifts to simple linear regression, where the lengths are used to predict a variable H, and the residuals are calculated to build the cost function. This cost function has two inputs, theta zero and theta one, leading to a three-dimensional cost landscape. The process involves plotting various values for these parameters to evaluate the cost surface.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What you are also seeing in this last function is that this is a convex function, so it curves upward, meaning that if I go ahead and take the derivative and find the optimum point. When the derivative is equal to zero, that's the point that I have the minimum. That's exactly what's happening here That's why I was always taking the derivative So if I take the derivative and I look at the minimum point the minimum point should be this point And I care again less about what value of loss this corresponds to but more about what is the tetra zero hat value It looks like that it's just slightly above 10, so we'll take a look at that number So if I go ahead and now move on to this simple linear regression, we're taking a look at the lengths and use the lengths in order to predict the H and then we're calculating the residual in the H prediction and just building the cost function like that. So now my cost function you're seeing that it has two inputs. It has t to zero and t to one. In the constant model example, the inputs to the function was only one. So effectively, when I was plotting function R, I had t to zero on the x axis as the input and then I had R on the y axis. In this case, I have t to zero and I have t to one and then that's that's two dimensions. And then if I want to plot also the cost value, I need to go to the third dimension. So that's why the cost landscape or the last surface is going to be a tree dimensional function. I'm going to do exactly the same thing. Again, picking a bunch of different values for theta zero and a bunch of different values for theta one and then just building the cost surface or building a model evaluating a cost. This is going to be the fancy lost landscape. Predimensional space. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

# Just run this cell
import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0], data_linear. iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np. linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')

# Just run this cell
import itertools
import plotly.graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear.iloc[ :, 0], data_linear.iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x)) ** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np.linspace(-80, 20, 80) ]
theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')
# Optimal point
data_x, data_y = data_linear.iloc[ :, 0], data_linear.iloc[ : , 1]
theta_1_hat = np. corrcoef(data_x, data_y) [0, 1] * np.std(data_y) / np.std(data_x)
theta_0_hat = np.mean(data_y) - theta_1_hat * np.mean(data_x)
fig_lin = go. Figure(data=[go. Surface(x=theta_0_values, y=theta_1_values, z=mse_values, name='z=MSE' )])
fig_lin.add_trace(go. Scatter3d(x=[theta_0_hat], y=[theta_1_hat], \
z=[mse_linear(theta_0_hat, theta_1_hat, data_linear) ], \
mode='markers', marker=dict(size=12), name='theta hat'))
fig_lin.update_layout(
autosize=False,
scene = dict (
xaxis_title='theta_0',
yaxis_title='theta_1',
zaxis title='z=MSE' ),

import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear.iloc[ :, 0], data_linear.iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np.linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np. linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear (theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np. reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F')

import itertools
import plotly. graph_objects as go
def mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear):
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0], data_linear.iloc[ :, 1]
return np.mean(np.array([(y - (theta_0+theta_1*x))
** 2 for x, y in zip(data_x, data_y)]), axis=0)
theta_0_values = np.linspace(-80, 20, 80)
theta_1_values = np.linspace(-10,30, 80)
mse_values = [mse_linear(theta_0, theta_1, data_linear) \
for theta_0, theta_1 in itertools. product(theta_0_values, theta_1_values) ]
mse_values = np.reshape(mse_values, (len(theta_0_values), len(theta_1_values)), order='F' )
# Optimal point
data_x, data_y = data_linear. iloc[ : , 0];
data_linear.iloc[ :, 1]
theta_1_hat = np. corrcoef(data_x, data_y) [0, 1] * np.std(data_y) / np.std(data_x)
theta_0_hat = np.mean(data_y) - theta_1_hat * np.mean(data_x)
fig_lin = go. Figure(data=[go. Surface(x=theta_0_values, y=theta_1_values, z=mse_values, name='z=MSE' )])
fig_lin. add_trace(go. Scatter3d(x=[theta_0_hat], y=[theta_1_hat], \
z=[mse_linear(theta_0_hat, theta_1_hat, data_linear) ], \
mode='markers', marker=dict(size=12), name='theta hat'))
fig_lin.update_layout (
autosize=False,

children.title: Understanding 3D Cost Surface for Optimization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on a color-coded cost value representation, where dark blue indicates low costs and yellow indicates high costs. The goal is to identify the optimum point on this 3D surface, which is visually represented. The optimum attributes are identified as theta_0 = -44, theta_1 = 23, and a cost value of 18, although the main interest lies in the theta values rather than the cost itself.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Right. It's also kind of color coded using this cost value. You see that when there is dark blue, that's when we have a low low cost. And when it's yellow, that's when we have a very large cost. So what we want to do is we want to be really down in the valley and we want to find out what is the optimum value that gives us that minimum minimum point. children.title: Calculating RMSE for Regression Models
children.summary: The cost of the model is emphasized, aiming for it to be minimized at an optimal level. The square root of the mean squared error (RMSE) is calculated for both the constant model and the simple linear regression model. The RMSE for the constant model is determined to be 7.7.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's interesting when you compare that 18 to this value right up here, which you see that the cost is equal to 12,000. So that's why we really want to make sure that the cost of the model is less and less and exactly at the optimum place that we want it to be. So once I build those models, okay, based on the Tetra 0 hat for the constant model and Tetra 0 hat in combination with Tetra 1 hat for the For the simple linear regression, I can go ahead and calculate the square root of the mean squared error, right? Mean squared error, we have it. I just take the square root of that. And you will see that the RMSE for the constant model is equal to 7.7. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab
