children.title: Comparison of Constant and Linear Regression Models
children.summary: The segment discusses the significant error in age calculation due to miscalculations, emphasizing the importance of using RMSE (Root Mean Square Error) for evaluating model accuracy. It compares a constant model, which provides a single average value, to a simple linear regression model, which yields a better estimate as indicated by a lower RMSE. The discussion includes how to visualize the results through plotting and understanding the loss landscape, highlighting the optimization process of minimizing the objective function based on varying model parameters.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: That's 7.7 years of miscalculation, because I was calculating the age of the do-gun, right? That's a pretty much large error. As opposed to when I was looking at the simple linear regression, the RMSE was slightly better. So this kind of tells you that when I'm looking at the error in the year unit is better than year score unit. So that's why we always calculate a score root of the minus score. A score root of the minus score error, which gives us the RMSC. If I go ahead and actually do some productions and take a look at those productions, let's see what we will get. So we had the constant model, I had all of those age values and definitely with the constant model, I can just draw a line or just the value, single value using a rock plot. So you would see that, okay, in this case, It is equal to the average of all of the data points that we had for some for most of them we have miscalculation but an average it will work out as the best only single estimate if I'm making simplifying assumptions. If I go ahead and do exactly the same thing with teta zero hadn't teta one hat and just draw a line for the simple linear regression you can see that it's a better estimate that's why we had a lower RMSC. Okay, so this is kind of a comparison of What happens when your model is simple what happens when you keep complicating your model and then what happens with the result how to show the result that you're getting how to plot the uh each one of those functions how to plot the loss landscape how to make sense of what's the dimensionality of your loss function and what did I mean by saying that this loss function or this cost or objective function is actually a function of theta because like In one domain, we're just changing different types of values. And by changing those types of values, you have a different objective value. And then you're basically trying to optimize to get to the minimum of that landscape. Video: 1: V

children.title: Mean of Y Values as Best Estimator
children.summary: The best estimator for a constant model with Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss is the mean of the Y values, which has been the focus of the discussion. A brief break is announced, encouraging students to watch a specific video for a refresher on algebra before the next lecture. Additionally, a comparison between constant and linear models is made, highlighting that the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for the constant model is higher than that for the linear model, indicating the constant model performs worse in this metric.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So, fun parts so far. So we're done with these demos. So you saw all of these things that we had seen. And this is another best light a question just to give you some breather before we move on to the next example. The question is the best estimator for a constant model with MSC loss is the mean of Y values. Sounds a little bit trivial. answer is yes. That's what we have been talking about the past 50 minutes. So when we have a constant model with MSD optimum point is that the optimum tets of zero hat value is actually the average of wise. So I'm going to give you two minutes of break. You deserve it. But this is also with the caveat that this is your take away for the next lecture. You're going to go ahead and watch this Tribal One Brown video to just get some refresher under your algebra before you come to the next lecture. Video: 1: Y

children.title: Convexity in Functions and MAE Model
children.summary: Concerns about determining convexity in functions were addressed, emphasizing the importance of checking the second derivative to confirm that the optimum point is indeed a minimum. The segment introduced the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) as a metric for model fitting, explaining it as the average of the absolute errors across all data points. The upcoming model will build on previous concepts, specifically transitioning from constant models with Mean Squared Error (MSE) to those utilizing MAE.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: But let's take it two minute break and then we'll be back with the next example. We're going back again. 5 4 3, 2, and 1. Olivia is the winner of the questions of today. Olivia is asking, do we know if a function is convex or not? When we're always assuming that, oh, you take the derivative of the set of equal to zero and you always say that that's minimum. and... That's a valid concern. We always need to check for the second derivative to make sure that the function is actually convex, meaning that the optimum point is always the minimum point. And I'm going to take a look at that together with you in a second in the next few slides. Okay. So the next model is going to be an easy model now that you've gone through doing constant model with msc doing constant model with ma is going to be easier. What was the MAE? MAE was mean absolute error. Remember that we had the last function, or two last, L1 last, this is L1 last, but taking average over all of the data points that we have, which we call it mean absolute error. So I'm going to take a look at how I can fit a model and how that looks like. But to develop it tricky, but I just want you to see it. Video: 1: V

children.title: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) Function Explanation
children.summary: The segment discusses the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) function, focusing on its calculation using the absolute value of residuals, which represents the L1 loss. It explains how to compute the average of L1 losses across all data points in the dataset. The objective function is introduced, highlighting its complexity compared to previous functions. A specific data point's L1 loss is analyzed, showing how the slope changes based on the value of theta zero. The overall MAE function is described as having a v-shaped structure for each data point, resulting in a piecewise linear function with kinks at specific data values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: one at least once. So this is MAE function. So we just plugged in. What did we plug in? We just have the residual, the absolute value of the residual risk, which is effectively my L1 loss. And what I'm doing is I'm taking average over all the L1 losses for all I values, an I from 1 up to N in the data set that has Y1 up to YN and different data points. If I'm saying that my model is a constant model, I just need to go ahead and replace why I have a tetasera because always it's going to be tetasero, so we might as well want to replace it there before we do any optimization. So this is the objective function. It looks a little bit scary, particularly because this function is slightly different than the function that we had seen before. So if I take a look at, for example, the L1 loss for a single data point. So what we would basically take a look at is that, okay, for this is specific data point, for different choices of teta 0 value, again, notice that teta 0 is on the x-axis. I have different loss values and it depends on whether it's 0 is less than 20 or above 20 because I have that absolute value sign so sometimes this slope is negative, sometimes this slope is positive. So that's basically the shape of this loss function for a single data point. When I want to consider all the data points that I have right here and I take the average over all of those absolute value of functions. Effectively, I'll get this M-A-E function, that the M-A-E function has one of those like v-shaped functions here for 20, one of those v-shaped functions for 21, for 22, 29, and 33. And then if I sum them up together and calculate the average, what I'll get is this kind of a He survives linear function that has multiple kinks and the location that it has kinks is that the point that we have each one of these individual data points in our data set right so we'd see that there is one at 20 there is one at 21 there is one at 22 and then 29 and then 33 are all of those like breaking points that we have. When I want to consider all the data points that I have right here and I take the average over all of those absolute value of functions. Effectively, I'll get this M-A-E function, that the M-A-E function has one of those like v-shaped functions here for 20, one of those v-shaped functions for 21, for 22, 29, and 33. And then if I sum them up together and calculate the average, what I'll get is this kind of a He survives linear function that has multiple kinks and the location that it has kinks is that the point that we have each one of these individual data points in our data set right so we'd see that there is one at 20 there is one at 21 there is one at 22 and then 29 and then 33 are all of those like breaking points that we have.

children.title: Analyzing Cost Function and MAE Derivatives
children.summary: The cost function is identified as convex and upward, with a minimum point at 22, differing from a previous minimum of 25. The discussion highlights the challenge of differentiability in the function, particularly with the absolute value component. The first step in addressing this is to take the derivative, which involves summing the derivatives of the L1 losses. The segment emphasizes the need for a workaround due to the non-differentiable nature of the absolute value function, setting the stage for further exploration.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So this is the cost function, final objective function. It's fine, it's a convex function, it looks upward, but the issue is that this function has a different minimum point. Remember that our minimum point before was 25, which was the average. In this case, my minimum point looks to be close to 22. So we need to calculate and see how this minimum point now is different and is at 22 as opposed to being at 25. There is a second issue that this function is not differentiable. So, um, we will see how we can deal with that. Okay, so let's take a look. We know what we should do. We use our calculus. We write this function. We would say that. Okay, the first step was taking the derivative of this function, which is this, which is this coefficient of 1 over n times the summation of this L1 losses. We know that we should, the first step is that we can turn it into the summation of those derivatives. So this is summation of all of these derivatives. But now we'll figure out that if I want to take the derivative of this absolute value function, this absolute value function is not differentiable. So we have to find a work-around for it. And that's the next thing that we'll do. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Transforming Absolute Functions in Calculus
children.summary: The discussion focuses on transforming an absolute function into a normal function by conditioning over different values of yi. This involves expressing the function as a summation of cases where yi is greater than, less than, or equal to a threshold (theta 0), allowing for the elimination of the absolute value. The process of differentiation with respect to theta 0 is introduced, noting that while the derivation may be complex, understanding the underlying concepts is essential.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: It's going to be a little bit kind of challenging, but it's not that challenging, so we can do it. So the first thing that I'll do is I'll say that what if I go ahead and turn this absolute function to a normal function by just conditioning over different y i values, I would say that what if I write this as one over n times the summation of i from one up to n of The cases that yi is greater than teta 0, the case that yi is less than teta 0, and the case that yi is equal to teta 0. In this case, I can get rid of the absolute value function. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Derivative Analysis with Cases for yi and teta0
children.summary: The derivative is undefined when yi equals teta0, which is not considered for data 100. For cases where yi is greater than teta0, the derivative is straightforward as the expression inside the absolute value is positive. Conversely, if yi is less than teta0, the expression is negative, requiring a multiplication by negative one. The overall derivative can be expressed as 1/n times the summation of the derivatives for both cases, leading to a structured approach for further calculations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So I would say that this is undefined. The derivative is undefined. And we're not going to consider the case that yi is exactly equal to teta 0 for the purpose of data 100. For the other two cases, when my eye is greater than 10 to 0, I can just directly say that, okay, my function now is going to be... the derivative of yi minus teta zero, right, with respect to teta zero. Exactly because yi is greater than teta zero. So whatever is inside the absolute value function is positive, so it will be just the same value. In the other case, that yi is less than teta zero, that whatever is inside the absolute value function is going to be negative. So then I take the absolute value, I should multiply it with a negative one. So what I'll do is I'll say that this in this case is equal to taking the derivative of Uh at zero minus y i. Right. So this way is kind of simple, is kind of separate, and I can move forward by taking the derivatives. So if I go ahead and take the derivatives, what will I get? So I'm taking the derivatives, this is equal to 1 over n times the summation of i from 1 up to n of, okay, what is the derivative of the first case? The case is yi greater than teta 0, second case yi less than teta 0. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Model Fitting Using Calculus Derivation
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of fitting a model using calculus, specifically focusing on differentiating a function with respect to a parameter. It emphasizes that while the derivation presented is complex and not expected to be generated by students, understanding the underlying concepts is important.

children.title: Derivative Calculation and Summation Explanation
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of taking the derivative of a function with respect to theta zero, identifying the coefficients of the terms involved. The first term's derivative results in negative one, while the second term's derivative yields positive one. The overall derivative is expressed as a summation of these values, leading to a formula that incorporates the average of cases where yi is greater than theta zero, highlighting the division of data points into two categories.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Okay, what is the derivative of the 41st case with respect to that 10-0? I'll take a volunteer from here. Tell me, what is the derivative of This function right here with respect to teta 0. Anyone is following, please. I'm hearing negative one, right? So negative one, because I have negative one. As the coefficient of teta zero and I'm only taking derivative with respect to teta zero so that's the only term that will show up. So here I will have negative one. What is the derivative of the second term in respect to theta zero? It is equal to one because that's the coefficient that I had, right? So this is the head to zero with respect to head to zero, so the coefficient is equal to one, so this will be equal to positive one. So this is kind of where I'm at. So I was taking the derivative. So now my derivative is now turning into summation of a bunch of plus and plus one and minus one values, depending on their condition. Right? So if I go ahead and want to write out what's happening here effectively what's happening here is equal to. Me saying that it's 1 over n times the summation of all of the cases that yi is greater than and pet the zero. negative 1 plus 1 over n times the summation of all of these other cases, not the entire set of cases that we had, because my data points are going to be divided into two options. children.title: Model Fitting Derivation and Calculus Concepts
children.summary: The discussion focuses on differentiating a function with respect to a parameter, theta. It explains that values greater than theta contribute negative one, while values less than theta contribute positive one. This leads to a cleaned-up version of the derivative expression. The segment emphasizes understanding the derivation process, indicating that while the specifics may be complex, the core concepts are essential for model fitting.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Either they're greater than theta 0 or they're less than theta 0. If they're greater than theta 0, they're going to be counted in this case. They're going to contribute negative 1. If they are less than theta 0, they're going to contribute positive 1. So that's why this is going to be positive one, but for which case, for the case is that why i is less than theta zero. That's my derivative. Okay. Sounds a little bit complicated, but things are going to get better and you might be guessing what's happening here. And I'm just using a different notation this time when I'm just doing the derivative. So this is where we're at, kind of cleaned up version. Video: 1: V

children.title: Model Fitting and Derivative Calculation
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of fitting a model by calculating derivatives. It involves setting the derivative equal to zero to find the optimal parameters, specifically theta zero hat. The discussion includes handling cases where theta zero hat is less than or greater than the observed values (yi), and the contribution of these cases to the overall summation. The derivative of the absolute value function at zero is noted as undefined, but it is stated that this does not affect the outcome of the derivation.

children.title: Determining Median as Optimal Value
children.summary: The discussion focuses on how the optimal value, denoted as teta 0 hat, can be determined by the median of a dataset. It explains that when teta 0 hat equals the median, the number of data points less than and greater than this value will be equal, effectively dividing the dataset into two equal halves. The segment emphasizes that this approach can be applied even with an undifferentiable function, ultimately leading to the conclusion that teta 0 hat represents the median value. Additionally, the implications of different objective functions and their influence on the choice of penalty for misproductions are briefly mentioned.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: This is this term that I move. This is equal to moving this other term to the other side. It's going to be equal to the summation of teta 0 hat less than y i. And this is the positive one, positive one. What does this tell you? It tells you that I'm counting a bunch of positive ones. And the number of positive ones that I'm counting in one side should be equal to the number of positive ones that I'm counting on the other side. Right? It's as if that I'm deciding about TET-0 and placing it somewhere that is going to divide my data set into two pieces. What kind of statistical metric or values going to divide your data setting to two equal pieces? Median is going to do that. Effectively, if my tetra zero hat is equal to median, the number of data points that are less than that and the number of data points that are more than that are going to be equal, and then that's my optimum point. And we arrived at it with an undifferentiable function, but we tried to be a little bit patient, but it encirculated under different circumstances, and then we arrived at the point that teta 0 hat is equal to median. So the rest is basically kind of exactly what I described that teta 0 hat is now going to be equal to the median value that we wanted. And if we go ahead and plot it on the The boba t example that's going to now be equal to 22. So again, this is an example that depending on your objective function and how you would want to penalize misproductions, you can literally get at any possible value, right? And the number of positive ones that I'm counting in one side should be equal to the number of positive ones that I'm counting on the other side. Right? It's as if that I'm deciding about TET-0 and placing it somewhere that is going to divide my data set into two pieces. What kind of statistical metric or values going to divide your data setting to two equal pieces? Median is going to do that. children.title: Loss Functions and Optimization Principles
children.summary: The discussion covers the use of Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Mean Squared Error (MSC) as loss functions, emphasizing their impact on determining optimum parameters. It is noted that for the first derivative to equal the minimum, the function must be convex; otherwise, it may represent a maximum or a saddle point. The importance of the second derivative is highlighted for confirming the nature of the critical points. The segment also mentions that the objective functions being defined are assumed to be convex and differentiable, allowing for effective optimization. A visual comparison of the effects of MAE and MSC on a constant model is planned.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And in this case, we use MAE or absolute loss or L1 loss and then we arrive at 22. In the previous case, we had MSC rewrite that demean of the distribution. So kind of summary of everything that we've seen. We talked about objective function. We talked about using different loss functions. And then what's the impact of having different loss functions in order to what final value of optimum parameters we're going to arrive at? So every time we calculated the derivative or partial derivative, we set it equal to zero, we solved it. We found that those optimum parameters. One thing that I didn't tell you, and I briefly told you is that the function needs to be convex for the first derivative to be equal to the minimum. Otherwise, if the function is concave, the optimum point is always going to be the maximum. Sometimes when you take the derivative and the derivative is equal to zero, that's a saddle point. So we always need to take the second derivative. That's also what you're going to get more practice in homework. Five. And just here, the assumption is that and we're going to show you with proof that all of these functions, objective functions that we're defining, we're defining a bit of the assumption that these functions are convex, and we're actually defining them in a way that the function should always be convex and differentiable so that we can actually take the derivative and do the optimization and so on and so forth and then find the optimum minimum point of those functions. So in this case what I'm going to do is just kind of visually Compare what happened with MSC, what happened with M A E with the constant model. Because we use the single model. The only thing that was different was just the loss function that we picked. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Comparison of MSE and MAE in Loss Functions
children.summary: The segment discusses Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) as loss functions used for optimization. MSE is defined as the average of the squares of the differences between predicted and actual values, minimized using the sample mean. Conversely, MAE is the average of the absolute differences, minimized using the sample median. A visual demonstration illustrates the optimal points for both loss functions, highlighting their respective behaviors in relation to data.

children.title: Comparison of MSE and MAE in Modeling
children.summary: The discussion highlights the differences between Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Mean Absolute Error (MAE), noting that the optimum for MAE is at the median, while for MSE it is at the mean. It emphasizes the characteristics of the loss functions, with MSE being smooth and differentiable, whereas MAE is piecewise linear and non-differentiable. The impact of outliers on modeling is examined, particularly how a significant outlier can affect the loss landscape and the choice of loss function. MSE penalizes larger miscalculations more heavily, which influences the model's predictions and balances smaller values against larger outlier values.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And then you saw that with the M A E, we had this other function that was piecewise linear and the optimum was at 22. So that was kind of the difference between when we were doing the MSE versus MAE, and we arrived at different tetasero hat values. One time the tetasero hat was mean, one time the tetasero hat was equal to median. The other considerations was that one function was smooth, it was differentiable, this other function was not smooth. And then it was piecewise linear. And we're going to use some of these techniques later on in order to see how we can deal with all of these piecewise linear non-differentiable functions that we're going to have in different machine learning models. One more consideration. Remember that we had a bunch of two data sets in an SCOM quartet data set that had outlier and then it was just kind of messing up with the entire modeling piece. We want to take a look at the impact of having an outlier and the choice of the loss function. So for example, in this case, you're seeing that I added a very large outlier data set, one thousand thirty trees, somehow you still have many properties in a day. And we're going to take a look at what that means to the Lost Landscape. Take a look at the Lost Landscape for MSC versus MAE. Remember that I was telling you that MSC is a function or the L2 loss is a function that penalizes larger miscalculations or mis-predictions more. So that's what's happening in this case. It's trying to really shift the model such that it's kind of a striking balance between predicting those smaller values that are close to each other and also having a little bit of mis-prediction for this large value so that it doesn't get a very huge escort loss for that outlier value. children.title: Comparison of MSE and MAE Sensitivity
children.summary: The Mean Squared Error (MSE) and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) are compared in terms of their sensitivity to outliers. MSE is minimized with the sample mean, while MAE is minimized with the sample median. The discussion highlights that when outliers are present, MSE is more affected, leading to a shift in the optimum point, whereas MAE, being based on the median, results in a less substantial shift. An example dataset is provided to illustrate these differences.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So that's why the tet is zero had in this case is equal to 293 as opposed to with the MAE loss is going to be more reliable. It's going to just slightly shift it again because it's just a median that's being shifted. You're expecting that the outliers are going to be on two ends. So it's just generally going to shift your optimum point as likely to the left or to the right, but not substantially to turn it into 190. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Comparing MSE and MAE Sensitivity
children.summary: Mean Squared Error (MSE) is more sensitive to outliers, while Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is more robust. In datasets with many outliers, one can either remove outliers or use a less sensitive loss function to avoid skewed predictions. The uniqueness of solutions differs between MSE and MAE; MSE always yields a unique solution as the average of data points, while MAE can result in multiple optimal solutions, especially with an even number of data points, leading to non-unique outcomes. Additionally, when plotting residuals, no patterns should be visible, indicating a good model fit.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So what I would say is that MSC is more sensitive to outliers, but MAE is more robust. So that's the difference. That's the choice. If you're thinking of your data, it has a lot of outliers. One thing is you remove outliers. The second thing is that use a A last function that is less sensitive so that your predictions are not going to be so off. The other thing is the uniqueness of the solution. Say if I add another sample to your dataset, previously you had five days, now I'm going to give you the sixth day. And I'm going to tell you that, okay, go ahead and calculate your optimum tetasero hat for those cases. What you're seeing here is that But the M&C I still have the unique solution, the unique solution is just the average of whatever data points that I have. So it's still going to be the average, it's likely going to be different depending on this new data points data point that I got. But with the M AE, what you're seeing is that because I have even number of data points, I have infinitely many optimum points between the two middle points. In practice, what we would say is that we would say that we're going to take a look at the middle of those points as the value that we can use, but just know that with the M.A.E. is possible that you might get a non-unique solution or you might have multiple different values that would have the same objective. function value that you can use so meaning that you have you'll have different different lines of the same quality So with those examples out of the way, I want to go through transformation one more time. Remember that I was telling you that, okay, when I'm plotting the residual plot, I don't want to see any pattern. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab

children.title: Transformations for Linear Regression Modeling
children.summary: Feature engineering is crucial for preparing data for linear regression modeling. If a pattern is observed in the data, it indicates that transformations may be necessary. The discussion revisits the Tukey-Mosteller bulge diagram, which aids in visualizing how to transform axes to achieve linearity between variables. In a practical example involving simple linear regression, the residual plot shows an upward trend, suggesting improvement over a constant model but indicating that further data transformation is needed for optimal modeling.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: If you see a pattern, that means that you have not done in a feature engineering, or your data was not ready for a linear regression modeling that you could do, that's the thing that we can fix with transformation. Hopefully by now, you're a little bit more comfortable with transformation. Remember what we had in transformation, which was that I had this 2K muscle or bulge diagram, or we had a way of thinking about how to transform different axes so that the data is linearized. So the transform version of x with respect to the transform version of y is showing a linear relationship, which means that at that point you can use a regression line or a regression modeling for your modeling purposes. So in the case of this new Gong example, I'll tell you I said that, okay, I'm doing a simple linear regression and I'm happy with the simple linear regression compared to the constant model. If you go ahead and take a look at the residual plot, the residual plot kind of cares upward. So, I'll tell you that this is better than the constant model. It's still not perfect. It still shows pattern. I still need to transfer my data to make it my data ready for the proper modeling. Video: 1: v

children.title: Data Transformation for Linearization
children.summary: To linearize data for modeling, consider transformations that match the pattern observed in the residual plot. Instead of modeling y directly with respect to x, take the logarithm of y, denoting it as z. This approach aims to create a linear relationship, allowing for effective modeling. The original data and its residual plot exhibit a clear pattern, suggesting that a transformation is necessary to fit a curve to the data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So how do I do that? I go back to this to Key-Masala-Balaj diagram and I would say that, okay, what kind of transformation would help me linearize my data? You would see that the pattern that I have in the data or in the residual plot is actually showing that it's a match with this order of this voltage diagram. And then what I can do is technically I can pick any of those transformations in order to linearize my data. So is we'll go back linearize our data once the data is linear linearized then we can go ahead and just do the modeling as well and this time hopefully the residual plot is going to be a very nice residual plot that we can use. So again, instead of modeling y with respect to x, meaning that I do y equal to theta 0 plus theta 1 times x, what I can do is I can say that I'm going to take the log of y. And I'm going to call log of y as a variable z for ease of use. And I would say that, okay, now I'm modeling z with respect to x. which Z is actually now going to be the log transform version of y which hopefully is going to linearize my data. So going back and taking a look at how it looks like. This is the original data, original line, original residual plot. If I go ahead and linearize it, you would see that now I have log of Z, log of h or log of y, or I'm calling it as Z. Video: 1: v

So again, instead of modeling y with respect to x, meaning that I do y equal to theta 0 plus theta 1 times x, what I can do is I can say that I'm going to take the log of y. And I'm going to call log of y as a variable z for ease of use. And I would say that, okay, now I'm modeling z with respect to x. which Z is actually now going to be the log transform version of y which hopefully is going to linearize my data. So going back and taking a look at how it looks like. This is the original data, original line, original residual plot. If I go ahead and linearize it, you would see that now I have log of Z, log of h or log of y, or I'm calling it as Z.

children.title: Log Transformation and Linear Regression Model
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the application of log transformation to improve linear regression modeling. It explains that a linear pattern in the residual plot indicates a better fit, suggesting that the log of the variable is a more appropriate transformation. The speaker describes a dataset relating lengths to age, noting that after applying the log transformation to age, the data became linear. The equation for the linear regression model is presented as y = e^(θ0 + θ1 * x), where x represents the length variable. This transformation results in an exponential function, demonstrating how the linear regression line adapts to the transformed data.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Which is which is showing a linear pattern with respect to the input so now the line passes through the data points a little bit more accurately and if I go ahead and take a look at the residual plot not the residual plot doesn't show any pattern. Which technically means that log of why is the better transformation to use with respect to the input x that I had and now linear regression model is going to be more appropriate otherwise if your residual plot shows the pattern that means that there is some pattern in the data that you you haven't captured so you have not done good enough of a job. So what does this mean? This means that okay, that was my data set, the data set of lengths with respect to age. I was not happy with just using this because I was seeing that the data set kind of cares upward. I did the transformation, I took the log of the age, so now I have log of the age, which I'm calling it as being equal to Z. And now the data is linear, I fit a linear regression line. So I have links with respect to log of age that I'm predicting, which again, this is the line and the equation for the line is going to be, for example, something as peta, I'm going to write it at the top. It's going to be equal to teta zero plus teta one times x, which x in this case is going to be my length variable. So what I can do next is that in order to go back to the original kind of scale of the data that I know x with respect to y, what I can do is I can Calculate e to the power of each side. So I can say I'm calculating e to the power of z, which is going to be e to the power of log of y, which is going to give me y, which now I'm going to have the model that is going to be equal to y, equal to e to the power of what I had here, e to the power of this equation that is theta 0 plus theta 1 times x. And now you can see that my linear regression line turned into this exponential function for me. And if I go ahead and plot the residual, now the residual actually is going to make more sense for me as well. Video: 1: Clec11 - JupyterLab

leaf_chunk.audio: Which is which is showing a linear pattern with respect to the input so now the line passes through the data points a little bit more accurately and if I go ahead and take a look at the residual plot not the residual plot doesn't show any pattern. Which technically means that log of why is the better transformation to use with respect to the input x that I had and now linear regression model is going to be more appropriate otherwise if your residual plot shows the pattern that means that there is some pattern in the data that you you haven't captured so you have not done good enough of a job. So what does this mean? This means that okay, that was my data set, the data set of lengths with respect to age. I was not happy with just using this because I was seeing that the data set kind of cares upward. I did the transformation, I took the log of the age, so now I have log of the age, which I'm calling it as being equal to Z. And now the data is linear, I fit a linear regression line. So I have links with respect to log of age that I'm predicting, which again, this is the line and the equation for the line is going to be, for example, something as peta, I'm going to write it at the top. It's going to be equal to teta zero plus teta one times x, which x in this case is going to be my length variable. So what I can do next is that in order to go back to the original kind of scale of the data that I know x with respect to y, what I can do is I can Calculate e to the power of each side. So I can say I'm calculating e to the power of z, which is going to be e to the power of log of y, which is going to give me y, which now I'm going to have the model that is going to be equal to y, equal to e to the power of what I had here, e to the power of this equation that is theta 0 plus theta 1 times x. And now you can see that my linear regression line turned into this exponential function for me. And if I go ahead and plot the residual, now the residual actually is going to make more sense for me as well.

children.title: Introduction to Regression Concepts and Terminology
children.summary: An overview of the upcoming lecture content, focusing on curve fitting using least squares regression. Key topics include the modeling process, evaluation of simple linear regression (SLR) models, and notation for multiple linear regression. Additionally, a note on terminology outlines equivalent terms used in regression analysis, such as features, outputs, weights, covariates, and predictors, with an emphasis on the most common terms for the course.

children.title: Introduction to Multiple Regression Analysis
children.summary: The next lecture will focus on using the same notation for inputs and features to predict a single output value. The goal is to find a model with parameters that minimize the objective function across the dataset, ensuring predictions align with ground truth values. The discussion will shift from one-dimensional feature space to multiple inputs, such as length and weight, leading to the formulation of a multiple regression model. This model combines various inputs into a linear regression line, calculating a weighted average of the inputs, parameterized by coefficients and a bias intercept.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So in the next lecture, what we're going to do is we're going to use the exact same notation that we had before. So the same notation, meaning that I have inputs and features that I want to use. Then I have outputs that I want to predict. So the output is a single value, but feature can be multiple different features. And I want to go ahead and Find a model or a set of set of parameters that's going to give me my predictions. And I'm going to go after finding the estimated parameters that are going to be the best case scenarios with the minimum objective function or minimum loss across the entire data set that matches my predictions well to the ground truth values. So what we will do actually is move away from only living in one feature space or one dimensional feature space or taking only one input. And we're going to move on to the case that we have multiple different inputs. So you might have length, you might have, for example, weight, you might have different attributes for an instance or an object in order to make a prediction. So in that case, what we would say is that our model is going to be a multiple regression line. Multiple inputs are going to be combined together. It's a still a linear regression line. And then we basically calculate a weighted average of all of those inputs in order to make a prediction. So again, our machine here compared to last time or compared to also this time is that we have this type of vector that is parameterized by that is parameterized by teta 0, parameterized by teta 1, and teta p assuming that you have p different features plus the biased intercept that you have. leaf_chunk.audio: So in the next lecture, what we're going to do is we're going to use the exact same notation that we had before. So the same notation, meaning that I have inputs and features that I want to use. Then I have outputs that I want to predict. So the output is a single value, but feature can be multiple different features. And I want to go ahead and Find a model or a set of set of parameters that's going to give me my predictions. And I'm going to go after finding the estimated parameters that are going to be the best case scenarios with the minimum objective function or minimum loss across the entire data set that matches my predictions well to the ground truth values. So what we will do actually is move away from only living in one feature space or one dimensional feature space or taking only one input. And we're going to move on to the case that we have multiple different inputs. So you might have length, you might have, for example, weight, you might have different attributes for an instance or an object in order to make a prediction. So in that case, what we would say is that our model is going to be a multiple regression line. Multiple inputs are going to be combined together. It's a still a linear regression line. And then we basically calculate a weighted average of all of those inputs in order to make a prediction. So again, our machine here compared to last time or compared to also this time is that we have this type of vector that is parameterized by that is parameterized by teta 0, parameterized by teta 1, and teta p assuming that you have p different features plus the biased intercept that you have.

children.title: Introduction to Multiple Linear Regression
children.summary: The segment introduces the concept of multiple linear regression, explaining how it calculates a weighted average of various features to predict an outcome. It describes the model as y = β0 + β1x1 + β2x2 + ... + βpxp, where parameters are represented by A = [β0, β1, ..., βp]. An example is provided involving predicting a cat's age based on features such as length and weight. The discussion emphasizes the need for a refresher in linear algebra to optimize multiple parameters, concluding with an invitation to return for further exploration of the topic.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: And what it will do is it will calculate evaded combination of all of them together. It basically says that you have teta 0. It is going to multiply feature 1 with the parameter that's associated with feature 1, which is teta 1, and then also the same thing with multiplying teta 2 we'll get to 2 or the vape for Feature 2 is going to be get to 2 multiplied with Feature 2, so on and so forth, up until the point that you get this final production. So the example could be that if you take a look at my cat and you want to basically take a look at different data points of my cat over multiple years and I have multiple different features for her. I have her lengths and I have her hair weight and I want to go ahead and predict the age of the cat. What I can do is I can basically calculate the weighted average of all of these factors together in order to do this final production. This is the final thing that we wanted to go over today. So we're going to go deeper into this multiple regression. We're going to try to optimize multiple parameters together. And that's why you need to refresh a little bit under linear algebra. Thank you so much for coming. I'll see you back on Tuesday. Video: 1: lec11 - JupyterLab
